rationale_for_baseline_tier: Known, treated autoimmune thyroid disease with a 3-day symptomatic flare. The acute red-flag screen on patient-obtainable data is negative — the patient is alert and conversational, breathing normally, not vomiting, and reports a recently normal thyroid test — which excludes the immediate life-threats (myxoedema coma, adrenal crisis). The source clinician's own disposition, after establishing stability, was GP review in 48 hours. The correct telehealth answer is therefore prompt in-person GP review (T2, 24-48 hours) with urgent repeat thyroid function tests plus mandatory danger-sign safety-netting — NOT baseline emergency escalation. Social isolation and ~24 hours of poor intake justify acting at the prompt end of T2 (or T3 same-day/home visit), but do not warrant baseline T4/T5. The narrow telehealth-limit concern (vitals cannot be measured) is handled by conservative danger-sign netting, not by flooring the baseline tier higher.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Becomes difficult to rouse, very drowsy, or cannot be kept awake
escalation_edges.rationale: Reduced level of consciousness in this context signals decompensated hypothyroidism (myxoedema coma) — a life-threatening emergency requiring ambulance and hospital rescue.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become so sleepy you can't stay awake, or someone can't wake you properly, call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New or worsening breathing difficulty, gasping, or very slow/shallow breathing; or feeling so cold you cannot warm up and becoming confused
escalation_edges.rationale: Respiratory depression or profound hypothermia with confusion indicates severe decompensation — an emergency; core temperature and breathing cannot be measured remotely, so any such change must be treated as urgent.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start having trouble breathing, or you feel dangerously cold and start getting confused, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep any fluids down or vomiting, not passing urine, feeling faint/like passing out, or new dizziness on standing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the emergency department the same day; call 000 if you feel faint or collapse
escalation_edges.rationale: Inability to maintain intake, reduced urine output or presyncope suggests dehydration or evolving decompensation (including possible adrenal involvement), which needs in-person assessment and bloods that telehealth cannot provide — heightened because the patient lives alone.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep any fluids down, you're being sick, you stop passing urine, or you feel faint — get to an emergency department the same day, and call 000 if you feel like you might pass out.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Symptoms clearly worsening over the next 24 hours (more exhausted, more cold, more foggy) without the emergency danger signs above
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day urgent GP or urgent care review
escalation_edges.rationale: A graded rung: worsening without emergency features still warrants prompt same-day review rather than waiting for the routine appointment, especially given unmeasurable vitals and social isolation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you're clearly getting worse over the next day but none of the emergency signs are there, see a GP or urgent care the same day rather than waiting.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one currently checking in, and has had poor oral intake for ~24 hours
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to act — recommend arranging someone to be present today/tonight, consider a same-day GP home visit or urgent care (upper end of T2 / T3), and make the danger-sign netting explicit because a deterioration could otherwise go unwitnessed.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known treated autoimmune thyroid disease; vitals cannot be measured via telehealth
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep the danger-sign edges (consciousness, breathing, intake) explicit and mandatory since early decompensation cannot be positively excluded remotely.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange to see your GP in person within the next 24-48 hours (sooner if you can, given you live alone), and ask for repeat thyroid blood tests. Keep taking your thyroxine at the usual dose. In the meantime, sip fluids and eat small amounts regularly, keep warm, and have someone check on you today and tonight.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call your GP clinic to book an urgent in-person appointment in the next 1-2 days; if after hours or you can't get in, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84). For any of the emergency danger signs, call 000.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours: 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for triage advice. For any danger sign — can't stay awake, trouble breathing, can't keep fluids down, confusion, or fainting — call 000 or go to the nearest emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the GP about the rapid 3-day decline, that your recent thyroid test was reportedly normal, your current thyroxine dose, and that you live alone; bring your medication details.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treats this as pure self-care with no medical review and no safety-netting — a rapid 3-day decline with unmeasurable vitals in someone living alone could mask early decompensation.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Patient stays home alone, deteriorates unwitnessed overnight, and decompensates with no one present to call 000.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine 1-2 week GP review under-rates the rapid decline, poor oral intake and social isolation, and delays the repeat thyroid testing that is needed promptly.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Reflexive emergency ambulance/ED as the baseline disposition when the patient-obtainable picture (alert, conversational, breathing normally, not vomiting) already excludes the immediate life-threat — over-medicalises a benign flare, causes an unnecessary and distressing emergency transport, and is exactly the over-triage this case is built to resist.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Temperature, heart rate and blood pressure cannot be measured via chat, so early decompensation cannot be positively excluded — but the patient being alert, conversational and breathing normally does exclude the immediate life-threats (myxoedema coma, respiratory failure).
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Recognise the benign flare on the patient-obtainable picture, arrange prompt in-person review with bloods, and attach explicit, mandatory 000 safety-netting for the danger signs — rather than either dismissing it or defaulting to an emergency ambulance.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either (a) 'I can't check your vitals so call an ambulance / go to ED now' as the baseline plan (over-triage), or (b) 'it's just your thyroid, rest up' with no safety-netting (false reassurance / under-triage).
